title: PM Modi Vows 'India Will Never Bow to Any Form of Terror' on Pahalgam Attack First Anniversary
detail: On April 22, 2026 — the first anniversary of the Pahalgam massacre — Prime Minister Narendra Modi paid solemn tribute to the 26 civilians killed at Baisaran meadow in 2025. In a widely circulated statement, Modi wrote: 'Remembering the innocent lives lost in the gruesome Pahalgam terror attack on this day last year. They will never be forgotten. My thoughts are also with the bereaved families as they cope with this loss. As a nation, we stand united in grief and resolve. India will never bow to any form of terror. The heinous designs of terrorists will never succeed.' Senior members of India's political leadership — including Home Minister Amit Shah, Defence Minister Rajnath Singh, Opposition Congress president Mallikarjun Kharge, and Jammu & Kashmir Chief Minister Omar Abdullah — all issued separate anniversary tributes affirming bipartisan unity against terrorism. Rajnath Singh declared India's response to terrorism 'firm, decisive and unwavering,' directly referencing Operation Sindoor as proof of that resolve. Kharge, speaking for the Opposition, said 'India stands united' on the anniversary, framing the day as a moment of national unity. Omar Abdullah addressed a memorial gathering in Pahalgam, laying a wreath at the newly unveiled monument. A full security grid remained in place across J&K, with the Baisaran meadow — the massacre site — remaining sealed while broader Pahalgam valley continued to see a revival in tourist footfall. Security forces noted that approximately 46 militants have been eliminated in J&K since the April 2025 attack, including the three direct perpetrators tracked and killed in Operation Mahadev.

media.caption: PM Modi pays tribute to 26 victims of Pahalgam terror attack on first anniversary, vowing India will never bow to any form of terror

title: Black Marble Memorial Unveiled on Lidder Riverbank for 26 Pahalgam Attack Victims on First Anniversary
detail: A permanent memorial was unveiled in Pahalgam on April 22, 2026, to honor the 26 victims of the 2025 terror attack — 25 tourists and local pony handler Adil Shah. Built of black marble along the banks of the Lidder River, the structure carries the engraved names of all 26 people killed in the Baisaran meadow massacre on April 22, 2025. Jammu and Kashmir Chief Minister Omar Abdullah attended the memorial ceremony and laid a wreath. The memorial stands as a tribute to the victims at the same hill resort where their lives were cut short by Lashkar-e-Taiba-linked Resistance Front militants. Pahalgam itself was described as witnessing a broad revival of tourist activity one year on — many visitors refusing to let the fear of terror define their travel choices — though the Baisaran meadow itself remained closed pending a full security and access review. News organizations noted that the memorial's construction, endorsed by the J&K government, was completed on schedule to coincide with the anniversary date. Security forces deployed around the memorial and across Pahalgam valley included Army, CRPF, BSF, and J&K Police under unified command — the same posture maintained since April 20 as part of the anniversary security grid.

media.caption: Black marble memorial unveiled on the banks of the Lidder River in Pahalgam, bearing the names of all 26 victims of the April 22, 2025 terror attack

title: India Inaugurates 'Human Cost of Terrorism' Exhibition at US Capitol Hill on Pahalgam Attack First Anniversary
detail: India's Ambassador to the United States Vinay Mohan Kwatra inaugurated 'The Human Cost of Terrorism' digital exhibition at Capitol Hill on April 22, 2026, timed precisely to coincide with the first anniversary of the Pahalgam massacre. The exhibition documents major global terrorist attacks — including the 1993 Mumbai bomb blasts, the November 2008 Mumbai attacks (26/11), and the April 22, 2025 Pahalgam massacre — and explicitly names Pakistan-based organizations and individuals responsible, including Lashkar-e-Taiba and The Resistance Front. The exhibition showcases Pakistan's terror ecosystem for a US political audience, with dossiers on organizations, funding chains, and named operatives. The diplomatic initiative was conceived in direct response to Pakistan's rising international profile following its facilitation of the US-Iran ceasefire (April 8, 2026) and hosting of the Islamabad Talks (April 11–12, 2026). India's strategy aimed to rebrand Pakistan in US political circles — specifically to counter any rehabilitation of Pakistan as a 'responsible broker' amid growing US-Pakistan diplomatic cooperation. Indian officials described the Capitol Hill initiative as part of a broader global campaign to maintain Western pressure on Pakistan over cross-border terrorism, following a similar Indian exhibition at UN headquarters in July 2025. Analysts described the timing as deliberate: India's public counter-narrative was launched on the same day as Pakistan's counter-media blitz framing India's Operation Sindoor as 'reckless escalation.'

media.caption: India inaugurates 'The Human Cost of Terrorism' exhibition at US Capitol Hill on the first anniversary of the Pahalgam massacre, targeting Pakistan-based terror networks
